productInfo.authorDescription: Larry Wilson is a pioneer in the area of Human Factors in safety.

narrationDetails.narration:
Hi, I'm Larry Wilson, the author of Safe start. Thanks for being here. 
We mentioned before that you'll be acting as a detective in this SafeStart journey. Why a detective? Because your life is full of clues that can help you figure out why you make errors and get injured.
As you become a better detective in this journey and in your life you'll become Safer at home, at work,and everywhere else you go. 
So get ready to look for clues and solve a few mysteries as you learn how to prevent errors and injuries.
narrationDetails.title: U1 Mysteries of Unit 1_12-04-2025

content.text: In this unit, we're going to explore 3 big mysteries...
Mystery #1
Do people actually get hurt by the things they think will hurt them?
Mystery #2
Does how we feel sometimes greatly increase the chances that we’ll make a mistake (and get hurt)?
Mystery #3
Which practical (and simple) techniques can cut our errors by 50% or more?
Now here’s a quick mystery for you:
How can you get from one mountain top to another without walking up or down?

moduleData.moduleName: The Mystery of the Rocking Snail

option.text:

narrationDetails.narration:
Hi, I'm Michael Edwards, but you might know me better as Eddie the Eagle. And this is my SafeStart story. Eddie the Eagle Edwards.

Eddie takes himself seriously,even if nobody else does. But the fans loved him for all the wrong reasons. His lack of money trainer kits and facilities, he was the great British loser, and most of all, the air of bumbling incompetence somehow epitomized by those famous pink glasses.

As some of you probably know, the most dangerous thingI've ever done is jump the 90 meter at the Calgary Winter Olympics.

I'd done the 70 meter before, but not the 90. Everybody else jumping there had been jumping for 20, 30 years.

They've done 20,000 jumps. So I was still very much a beginner and I had the opportunity to go for it.

And I did it. And I landed it and I didn't get hurt. And it was such an incredible feeling.

I jumped further than I've ever flown before. Eddie's British record jump of 71m landed him 58th place out of 58 competitors, 19 m behind the jumper placed 57th.

I think it was fantastic, an incredible feeling, but also an incredible risk.

Not landing that 90 meter can be bad, really bad.

But I landed it and I didn't get hurt.

However, a few years ago my wife wanted to go to a car boot sale. I didn't want to go. We argued about it, I lost, we went to this car boot sale And she was looking around and she found this beautiful snail rocking horse.

It's like a rocking horse, but it's in the shape of a snail.And it was for the kids, obviously.

And we had two little kids, and she thought it would be great for the girls, so we argued about it.

I lost again, we bought it, and I had to carry this snail rocking horse.

Not only out of this car boot, but I had to carry it across the carpark and it put it into my boots of my car and it was really embarrassing.

What usually happens when you make an error?

narrationDetails.narration:
Let's say that your toe catches on something — a crack in the pavement... uneven flooring... or a rug in the hallway... Maybe this will happen 100 different times in your life.
What's the most likely outcome? Probably nothing.
You may barely notice it as you keep walking. Or you might have a close call where you stumble, but you catch yourself before you fall.
But some of the 100 times, you might actually fall and get a minor injury, like a scrape on your hand or a bruised knee.
A couple of times you might fall and get a fairly disabling injury, like a strained muscle in your back or a broken wrist.
But it's also possible that one of the times, you might fall and hit your head on something hard or sharp and get a serious head injury... People have died from this kind of fall.

When you think about it, we've all been injured a lot during our lifetimes.
Are all of these injuries unique situations, or is there a pattern behind them?
You might remember that Larry Wilson — the creator of this methodology called SafeStart — talked with thousands of people about their injuries (over 20,000 people!).
He noticed the same pattern in almost every injury these people had.
And the pattern wasn’t very complicated...
Four states cause four critical errors which cause less risk to become more risk.
Larry called this the State-to-Error Risk Pattern.
State-to-Error Risk Pattern
Tap image to enlarge.
A quick example?
You're rushing at work...(state)
So you aren’t paying much attention to where you’re walking... (error)
You slip on a spill and pull a muscle in your back... (increased risk led to an injury)
Ouch!
The goal of SafeStart Digital is to manage these states and reduce errors.
State-to-Error Risk Pattern
Tap image to enlarge.

content.text: Remember the state-to-error risk pattern?
State-to-Error Risk Pattern
Tap image to enlarge.
These 4 states cause most of the injuries (and close calls) we have. Seriously.
Click on each state to learn more.

Rushing
Going too fast, going faster than you normally do or doing too many things at once.
Example:
Over-tightening a bolt when trying to get an important machine back in service quickly.
Example:
Racing to work after sleeping too late.
Frustration
Being annoyed or angry at people or conditions.
Example:
Pulling hard on a stuck drawer.
Example:
Tailgating a slow driver on your way home from work.
Fatigue
Being too tired (physically or mentally) to do tasks safely.
Example:
Reacting slowly to something unexpected.
Example:
Forgetting an important step in a process.
Complacency
Being familiar enough with the hazards to feel less concerned over time.
Example:
Texting while walking (or driving!).
Example:
Fixing a machine without turning off the power to it.

Let’s look a little closer at the state-to-error risk pattern.
The 4 states lead to 4 critical errors — the ones that can really get us hurt.
State-to-Error Risk Pattern
Tap image to enlarge
Almost every injury (or close call) involves at least 1 of these 4 errors.

Eyes not on task
Not looking at what you are doing or not seeing what's going on around you.
Example:
Being distracted by something when riding your bike.
Example:
Not looking where you’re putting your hands.
Mind not on task
Not concentrating on what you are doing (even if only for a moment).
Example:
Thinking about a big bill you need to pay soon while fixing something.
Example:
Daydreaming of your next vacation while working in the garden.
Line of fire
Being in the path of hazardous energy or moving into its path.
Example:
Walking under a raised load.
Example:
Stopping in front of a busy entrance to look at your phone.
Balance, traction, grip
Doing something that causes you to slip, trip or fall; losing your grip on an object; or losing control of your vehicle.
Example:
Leaning too far over on a ladder.
Example:
Tripping on a step and stumbling.

Risk is an important part of safety.

narrationDetails.narration:
Everything we do involves some risk. from making breakfast... to driving to work... to doing projects at home.

But it's not just what we do; it's also HOW we do something that can increase or decrease this risk.

There are 3 factors that affect risk:
The amount of energy involved...
Not using protection...
and making errors.

We'll talk about energy in this module.

In a previous Bonus Module, we said there are 3 factors that increase risk:
-the amount of energy involved
-not using protection
-making errors.
In this module, we’ll talk about protection.
When we know an activity is risky, we often use protection, right?
If we ride a motorcycle, we put on a helmet.
If we do a dangerous task at work, we use protective devices, barriers and personal protective equipment.
So, if we do activities with little or no protection, how does that affect our risk?
It increases it!
Think about riding a motorcycle without a helmet. Very risky!

But I was fortunate. I had just passed another vehicle and when he looked in his rearview mirror, he did not see my tail lights. So he turned around and went back
and investigated, and he found the car way down where it was not visible from anybody on the street, and contacted us 911 and got the ambulance

I was in a wheelchair for six months. It could have been worse. I think, if he had not found me.

They, say that I would have died because of the blood loss and the trauma, and because it would have been difficult for anybody to find me.

narrationDetails.narration:
If you catch yourself rushing, ask yourself: Do I really need to be rushing right now?" 
Sometimes we get stuck in a mindset of doing everything fast when we actually don't need to.
The moment you realise you're rushing, slow down if possible. 
Think about it: are the few seconds or minutes you might save really worth the extra risk?

Another tip is to prioritise tasks. 

Make a list of what you need to do and finish the most important things first. 

It's stressful to run out of time because you've been sidetracked by less important tasks.
Along the same lines... 
Is something taking up a lot of your time but gives you little value? 
Many people browse social media or the internet to relax, but don't actually feel very good doing it.
Another tip? Stop multi-tasking. 

Science tells us our brains can only focus on one thing at a time. 
So multi-tasking actually means your brain is switching back and forth rapidly (not doing 2 things at once). 

This results in not doing either task very well, and feeling rushed in the process.

Finally, can you plan ahead? 

For example, if you're always rushing in the morning, can you do a few small things the night before that will free up time in the morning?

Pack your lunch...?

Get your clothes ready...?

Set out breakfast dishes...?

What do you think? What can you do to plan ahead and avoid rushing?
narrationDetails.title: U1 Rushing Tips_09-04-2025

narrationDetails.narration:
If you notice you’re getting frustrated… pause and take 5 slow, deep breaths.

When you breathe deeply (filling your belly) and exhale slowly, your nervous system sends a message to your brain to relax.

Your brain then notifies the rest of your body to do the same.

Here’s another tip.

Negative self-talk can increase your frustration level.

So, try to replace negative thoughts with positive ones.

For example, if you’re thinking, “This is making me crazy!”, then instead tell yourself “I’m good at this. I’ve been able to solve other problems, so I can figure this out too.

Sometimes just talking about your frustration with a calm loved one, friend or colleague can help.

Maybe you just need to vent. But avoid talking about it over and over.

Put your energy into finding solutions.

Sometimes we can get stuck complaining, when it would be more helpful to think of possible answers to the problem.
If you can’t solve a work situation on your own, talk to a supervisor.
When possible, try to bring a solution to the conversation.

Finally, look for a pattern behind your frustration; then prepare. Can you identify situations that regularly lead to frustration?

What can you do to avoid them or make them less aggravating?
narrationDetails.title: U1 Frustration Tips_11-04-2025

narrationDetails.narration:
Any advice about handling fatigue usually starts with “Get more rest.” But how do you actually do this?

Well, you can make sleep a priority. This may sound too general,but it’s really important. When you set an intention to get more sleep, you are more likely to make decisions that will lead to actually getting more rest.

Regular exercise – even just walking – really can help you sleep better. And if you get more rest,  you’re more likely to feel like exercising the next day.

It’s a win-win situation. Just be aware that for most people, it’s best to avoid vigorous exercise in the hour right before bed time.

Create a soothing bedtime ritual to decrease your stress level.

Turn off phone notifications...

Take a warm bath...

Enjoy a comforting cup of decaf tea...

Or listen to calming music…

Think about what’s relaxing for you. Here’s another tip: limit screen time in the 30 minutes before bedtime.

Listening- to a meditation app can be helpful, but scrolling through social media... playing a violent video game... or watching a stimulating programme
can make it more difficult to fall asleep or get good quality sleep.

You’ve probably heard this tip before: avoid alcohol and caffeine before bedtime, since both disrupt sleep.

Here’s one last tip... 

Try to go to bed  and wake up at the same time every day(even on weekends).

This helps to reinforce your body’s sleep-wake cycle.
narrationDetails.title: U1 Fatigue Tips_04-04-2025

narrationDetails.narration:
At this point, you know that complacency is not like the other 3 states.

Most of the time, we don’t even realise we’re complacent, since it happens gradually. Over time we stop being concerned about the dangers of an activity.

In SafeStart, we fight complacency by using the 4 critical error reduction techniques.

You’ll learn more about these in this unit and in the next 3 units of SafeStart.

One technique we’ll spend some time exploring in Unit 3 is building better habits.

Habits are actions we do without thinking about them.

So, if we create safer habits, we’ll be safer when our minds wander. And our minds will get distracted often.

We’re human, after all. So what can you do right now, today?

You can start asking yourself: “What could go wrong?”

Let’s think about an example. 

Let’s say you need to use a ladder.

You ask yourself “What could go wrong?” and think… If I don’t put the ladder on even ground, it could fall over…

If I climb too fast, my foot could slip… And if I lean too far over,the ladder could tip over.

By asking “What could go wrong?”, you can remind yourself to make sure the ladder feet are on level ground... to climb at a moderate pace... and to not lean too far to the side.

As you approach your next task today, ask yourself “What could go wrong?”

You may start to see things differently.

Even small things.

For example, a piece of paper on the floor is no longer just a piece of paper; it’s a slip hazard.
narrationDetails.title: U1 Complacency Tips_09-04-2025

narrationDetails.narration:
Hi, my name is Chris Bombani. I’m originally from Congo in Africa. And, now live in Scotland. I’m working for DSM nutritional products in Dalry, as instrument control engineer.  
My SafeStart story is from a few months ago, when I just started working for the company.  And at the same time, I just moved into my new home with my girlfriend. 
During this period, I was also writing my MSC dissertation.  
So I was spending a lot of time studying in home or in university. Because of my heavy schedule, my partner did most of the chores in our new home. On this occasion, because I was studying,she decided, she wanted to go for the night. So, I agreed to drop her and her two friends into town. 
When, I go home, I continued to work on my dissertation for a couple more hours. Eventually, though, I got really tired. So, I decided to stop for the night and make my lunch for the next day. 
When I walk into the kitchen, I noticed my girlfriend and her friends left the dishes everywhere. And then, when I realized how much more dishes there were in the sink my exhaustion just turned to frustration.

content.text: What do you think happens to Chris?

option.text: When he lifts the dishes out of the sink, a knife hidden under the plates cuts his hand badly. He needs surgery to repair a tendon and is out of work for 4 weeks.
He becomes more and more angry as he washes dishes, splashing water everywhere. He slips on the wet floor and strains a muscle in his leg.

After washing the dishes, he is extremely tired. While he dries and puts the dishes away, he leaves the cupboard door open. He turns and hits his head on the edge of the door and gets a bad cut (and a headache).

narrationDetails.narration:
When I, went to lift the dishes out of the sink, I didn’t notice there was a sharp knife in between the plates. 
I felt a sharp pain in my thumb. And, as I pulled my hand out,I realized I was bleeding. At first, I didn’t know how 
severe my cut was. I just seen blood. So, I got the kitchen cloth and wrapped around my hands to stop the bleeding. 
I didn’t notice, I couldn’t move my thumb up or down. And, that’s when I realized the cut was actually more severe, than I initially thought.

I called a taxi to take me to the accident emergency department at the hospital. When I arrived at the hospital, a nurse looked at my thumb and she advised me to get surgery on it to repair the severely damaged tendons. The surgery was successful. However left me with a scar and nerve damage to my thumb. 
My hand was immobilized in a splints following surgery. 

Well! my Tendon repaired. I was off for four weeks, and missed out lots of training and development due to the injury. And, also had to get an extension for my dissertation report.  
It could have been worse; the cut could have been more severe and caused permanent damage. 
I could have sliced more fingers, my hand or even sliced my wrist. 
As it was, I was lucky that the tendon could actually be repaired. Especially, because I’m right hand.
It could have so easily been prevented. 
Thinking first, before placing my hand on the bowl. I might have seen the knife sticking out.

And, I could have worn gloves that could gave me better protection. 
I have learnt from this, so regardless of tired, frustrated or whatever else I’m feeling. I will always stop and think before doing anything. Even if it is something as simple as washing the dishes. 
Because, it can still cause severe injuries and unnecessary setbacks.

You might be thinking that this pattern is just common sense.
For a lot of people, it is.
But we’ve all learned this the hard way — usually by getting hurt or having a close call that scared us.
Wouldn’t you prefer to not make errors and not get hurt?
If you think about the states and errors as the problem, then the critical error reduction techniques are the solution.

content.text: Next
Look at others for the patterns that increase the risk of injury
When you look at other people, you learn from what they’re doing.
You also watch to see if their actions at the moment could put you in danger.
Analyse close calls and small errors
When you analyse close calls, you learn from your small mistakes so you can prevent bigger ones.
Practise building habits
When you practise building habits, you develop better habits that will help you avoid critical errors.
Self-trigger on your state (or the amount of hazardous energy)
When you self-trigger, you recognise the state you are in or the amount of hazardous energy around you.
If this seems like a lot of information, don’t worry! You don’t have to memorise them.
You’ll learn more about each of the techniques throughout the 4 units of SafeStart Digital.
We’ll take our time, and you’ll practise each one to get familiar with it.
And that’s the most important part — practising.
The more you practise, the more likely you’ll be able to use these techniques in the moment you need them.

narrationDetails.narration:
Hi, my name is Shein Armaan and I own a couple of, sports bar and grills. And I'm going to tell you
about an accident.

That happened to me about six months ago. We had a big Pay-Per-View, boxing match, and I was working the night before at the restaurant, and I got to be at about four in the morning.

The next day, I woke up very early because I had to open up the restaurant, prepare for the, event.

Unfortunately, the weather was really bad, and, the satellite went out and we had already paid the Pay-Per-View, and we expected, about 200, 300 people.

And I called the cable company, and, they let me know that on a Sunday they cannot show up on such short notice.
 
So I had to get this fixed. Now we have a two story restaurant, and the satellite is on the roof of the second level.

The only one on location. And obviously this is a two man job because somebody has to adjust the satellite and somebody else has to look at
the picture and see if we have a picture.

Now I'm very tired because I'm working on three hours of sleep and I had to take care of this situation.

So I got one the second level with a toolbox and, tried to fix the cell on myself, and I was the only one here, so I had no help And because I was alone, you know, I'm rushing back and forth from the second level all the way down to this restaurant to try and see if we have a picture.

option.text: How could the situation have been worse?

narrationDetails.narration:
Hi, I’m Zacary Moxley. I’m an action/sport cinematographer. And here’s my SafeStart story.

Although I’m not exactly old, I’ve been filming freestyle skiing since I was 12.

I’ve worked with the BC Freestyle Team, the Canadian Freestyle Team, ESPN, X Games, Monster and even Red Bull.

Outside of skiing, I’ve shot downhill mountain biking, skate boarding, surfing, and I’ve also been hired to shoot rock concerts and large scale music festivals.

But the coolest thing I’ve done is aerial-cinematography. I have to follow skiers and snowboarders off of 50 foot jumps, downhill mountain bike at high speed, skateboard at high speed and even hang out of planes putting myself at lots of risk.

But here’s the thing, the worst injury I’ve ever had didn’t come from doing any of these dangerous activities,  it came from doing the dishes.

So what happened was I was drying a bowl, talking to my roommate, not really paying attention.

I mean, I’ve dried a millions dishes—and I dropped the bowl.

I tried to catch it, but it broke on the counter just as I was reaching for it. And I slammed my wrist into the broken pieces, and it cut my wrist.

I looked at it, and it was pretty bad, so I wrapped it up and I went to the clinic.

I got eight stitches, but it could have been worse. I was lucky. I didn’t cut any veins or arteries, and the broken edge could’ve cut a tendon.

It was really sharp.
 
It just seems ironic,
narrationDetails.title: U1 Zachary Moxley_22-07-2025

content.text: So, what does it mean to “look at others for the patterns that increase the risk of injury”?
The “pattern” mentioned here is the state-to-error risk pattern:
State-to-Error Risk Pattern
Tap image to enlarge.

narrationDetails.narration:
The idea is that you actively observe others to see if they are in one (or several) states or making any critical errors.

It's easy to observe the people around you – at work... at home... in your community... wherever you go. 

Is anyone rushing, frustrated, tired, or are in a world of their own?

When looking for critical errors, you might see people not looking where they are going 

...not thinking about what they are doing
...in the line-of-fire
...or losing their balance, traction or grip.

As you start observing other people, an interesting thing happens: you start seeing these behaviours everywhere, all the time. 

And what we see can actually help us a lot.
narrationDetails.title: U1 Look at Others CERT_12-04-2025

content.text: Observing other people is a “free” learning experience.
By watching others, we automatically bring ourselves back into the moment — it brings our eyes and mind back on task.
So in that moment, we can decide not to make the same mistake.
We may even stop doing the behavior in the future after we realise it's a risky thing to do.
Why don't you take a minute every now and then to look at others until it becomes a real habit?
And, of course, if you're looking at what other people are doing, you can get out of their way in time if needed!
If appropriate you might even step in to prevent someone from making a serious error.
